headline: Recess holds the docket still; New York's pocket-listing and algorithm-pricing bills move into position, and the federal foreign-buyer restriction cluster expands without consolidation.
paragraphs: No bill status changes appeared in today's data refresh — the summer recess continues to hold all tracked jurisdictions static. The near-term work is implementation: HUD rulemaking on the 21st Century ROAD to Housing Act (P.L. 119-101, signed July 11) is the critical milestone for developers and lenders with FHA-backed multifamily pipelines, as the law expanded loan limits, broadened HOME eligibility, and eliminated the permanent-chassis requirement for manufactured homes. Florida's constitutional property-tax overhaul (HJR 1-F, signed June 16) is now a ballot campaign rather than a legislative question; lenders underwriting Florida income-producing properties should model the assessment-cap tightening, homestead-exemption increase, and county ad valorem-tax limitation provisions before November, when a voter yes cements the framework into the state constitution.

New York's regulatory pipeline is the most active state-level watch heading into fall. A10679 — which would require prompt public marketing of listed residential properties and allow off-MLS marketing only with the seller's informed written consent after a state-mandated disclosure — was returned to the Assembly on June 1 after Senate passage, with S10274 simultaneously substituted by A10679B to consolidate the vehicles; a final-passage play is in reach for an early fall session. Also pending in the Assembly's housing committee is A11490, which would ban algorithmic and dynamic pricing tools in residential sales and rentals — a direct regulatory answer to revenue-management software controversies and a compliance trigger for any property manager using automated rent-setting systems. The technical good-cause-eviction fix (S08612) was referred to the Assembly Codes Committee on June 2; its enactment would resolve ambiguities in which buildings are covered and how rent increases are calculated under New York's 2024 statute, clearing a source of active litigation and lender underwriting uncertainty on covered portfolios.

At the federal level, the foreign-buyer restriction universe now spans at least six distinct bills with no floor votes scheduled and no committee consolidation in sight. SB176 would direct the President to prohibit all Chinese citizen and entity purchases of U.S. real estate outright; HB4706 and SB2258 would enact the same prohibition by statute; HB8700 would expand CFIUS jurisdiction over land near sensitive sites and broaden the definition of sensitive sites; HB3588 would impose a new tax on real estate purchases by non-citizens from countries that restrict U.S. ownership and require IRS reporting on all non-citizen transactions; and SB2573 directs a GAO study on the scope of foreign residential acquisitions. The bills span Foreign Relations, Agriculture, Financial Services, Ways and Means, and Banking committees. The aggregate trajectory — six bills across two chambers and five committees, with bipartisan sponsorship — makes sustained beneficial-ownership and nationality screening the prudent standard for any transaction that could involve foreign-linked capital, even while near-term enactment risk remains limited.
